revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11602924]] Wikidata name: Tsukushi script [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Chikushi Moji.jpg|thumb|Illustration of the mural in the Shigesada Kofun]] {{ill|Tsukushi script|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukushi script|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (つくしもじ) are a form of [[:en:Jindai moji|Jindai moji]] said to be inscribed on the stone chamber of {{ill|Shigesada Kofun|ja|重定古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigesada Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ukiha, Fukuoka|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukiha, Fukuoka|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Japan. They are also referred to as Shigesada Cave Ancient Characters (Shigesada Sekkutsu Koji, '''重定石窟古字''').
== Overview == {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirata Atsutane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} recorded these characters in his 1819 ({{ill|Bunsei|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunsei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 2) book *{{ill|Shinji Hinomibumi-den|ja|神字日文伝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinji Hinomibumi-den|qq=|1=Shinji Hinomibumi-den|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589447}}* (神字日文伝), listing them as "Stone Cave Characters of Chikugo Province." According to Hirata, there was a local tradition that referred to them as "characters from the age of the gods." However, he noted, *"It is difficult to ascertain whether they are truly characters."* The drawings of these inscriptions published in his work were originally obtained from a scholar of the {{ill|Kurume Domain|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurume Domain|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, passed on to Hirata through the Kokugaku scholar {{ill|Yashiro Hirokata|ja|屋代弘賢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashiro Hirokata|qq=|1=Yashiro Hirokata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6538130}}.
Due to Hirata's influence, interest in the inscriptions grew. When {{ill|Inō Tadataka|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inō Tadataka|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} visited Chikugo Province for surveying, he also investigated the Shigesada Kofun, conducting a detailed examination. This research, along with that of the Kurume Domain scholars, remains a valuable resource due to the scarcity of early documentation on decorated kofun.
On April 22, 1825 (Bunsei 8, 3rd month, 5th day), Kurume Domain retainers Murakami Yasutoshi and Hayakawa Issei created a detailed copy of the inscriptions. This illustration was later included in the work *{{ill|Nihon Kodai Moji Kō|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kodai Moji Kō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}* by the Kokugaku scholar {{ill|Ochiai Naozumi|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochiai Naozumi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Ochiai theorized that the script was formed by combining four types of symbols with eleven additional symbols. He also noted that some dot-like marks resembled the diacritic dots used in {{ill|Siddhaṃ script|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Siddhaṃ script|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, used for indicating long vowels. Additionally, historian {{ill|Kamiya Yoshimichi|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Yoshimichi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} pointed out in his 1886 (Meiji 19) article *Kodaiji Kō* (*古代文字考*), published in the *Journal of the Tokyo Anthropological Society*, that similar inscriptions were found on a stone from the residence of {{ill|Matsuura Takeshirō|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuura Takeshirō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (formerly part of a castle wall), as well as on the {{ill|Temple bell|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple bell|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of {{ill|Takase Shrine|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takase Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Tonami District, Etchū Province (modern-day {{ill|Nanto, Toyama|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto, Toyama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
The *{{ill|Takenouchi Documents|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Documents|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}* mention these inscriptions as "Yasoyo Characters" (ヤソヨ文字). According to *Shindai no Bankokushi* (神代の万国史) by {{ill|Takenouchi Yoshimiya|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Yoshimiya|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, these characters were created during the reign of the 22nd emperor of the Ninth Ancient Dynasty. *Nippon Ji no Hakkutsu* (につぽん字の発掘) by {{ill|Sakai Yoshio|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Yoshio|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} claims they originated in the era of Emperor *Amanohihirumotoashikabikikami*, the first emperor of humanity. (For more information on the emperors mentioned in the Takenouchi Documents, see {{ill|Takenouchi Documents' List of Emperors|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Documents' List of Emperors|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.)
Modern research considers these "Chikushi Characters" to be murals rather than syllabary-based writing, similar to the case of the so-called {{ill|Temiya Cave inscriptions|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temiya Cave inscriptions|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hokkaido. {{ill|Hara Masaru|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Masaru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in *Illustrated Guide to Shindai Characters* (*図説神代文字入門*), states: *"The attempts of Hirata and Ochiai were, in a way, akin to modern archaeology, as they sought to decipher messages left by ancient people."*
== Shigesada Kofun == [[File:Shigesada Kofun.jpg|thumb|Information board explaining the Shigesada Kofun. The top left shows a quiver (yuki), while the bottom left shows concentric circles.]] {{ill|Shigesada Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigesada Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} located on the left bank of the {{ill|Chikugo River|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo River|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is part of the {{ill|Asada Kofun Group|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asada Kofun Group|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and its construction is dated to the latter half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/6th century|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. On March 8, 1922 ({{ill|Taishō era|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō era|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 11), it was designated a {{ill|National Historic Site (Japan)|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Historic Site (Japan)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} along with the nearby Kusuna Kofun.
The front part of the mound has been lost, so its current length is 51 meters, but it is estimated to have originally been around 80 meters. The rear circular section has a diameter of 44 meters and a height of 8.5 meters. The rear section contains a stone chamber measuring 17 meters in length and 3.8 meters in height, connected to the exterior by a 6-meter-long passage. The chamber is notable for its use of massive andesite stones, with single large slabs forming the back wall, side walls, ceiling, and stone shelves.
The chamber walls are decorated with red and green pigments depicting {{ill|quivers (yuki)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/quivers (yuki)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (containers for carrying arrows), concentric circles, and fern-like motifs. In the rear chamber, quivers and a few concentric circles dominate, while the front chamber primarily features concentric circles, indicating a distinct pattern distribution between the two sections. These murals, composed of quivers and concentric circles, are what were interpreted as "Chikushi Characters."
The inscriptions and murals have been damaged due to various factors. During World War II, the tomb was used as an air raid shelter, and in the postwar period, a surge of interest in archaeology led to increased foot traffic, further eroding the markings. In 1962 (Shōwa 37), Tokyo University of the Arts professor Kusaka Hakkō created a restoration drawing (partially reconstructed). Copies of this restoration are preserved at the Ukiha City Museum of History and Folklore, and an enlarged version is displayed at the entrance of the Ukiha City Library.
Local folklore states that numerous earthenware vessels and ironware were once stored in the tomb. It is said that the local people would borrow these objects when needed, but when greedy individuals failed to return them, they disappeared.
Another kofun in Fukuoka Prefecture, the {{ill|Ōzuka Kofun (Katsuragawa, Fukuoka)|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōzuka Kofun (Katsuragawa, Fukuoka)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Katsuragawa, Fukuoka|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuragawa, Fukuoka|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, features murals similar to those of the Shigesada Kofun, including quivers and concentric circles. Decorated kofun of this type are concentrated in northern Kyushu.
== References == * {{ill|Ochiai Naozumi|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochiai Naozumi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, *Nihon Kodai Moji Kō* (日本古代文字考), Yoshikawa Hanshichi, Komakisha, 1888. {{NDL|53013099}} * {{ill|Ago Kiyohiko|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ago Kiyohiko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, *Nihon Shindai Moji: Kodai Waji Sōkan* (日本神代文字-古代和字総観), Tairiku Shobō, 1975. * Ago Kiyohiko, *Nihon Shindai Moji Kenkyū Genten* (日本神代文字研究原典), Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-404-02328-5 * {{ill|Hara Masaru (writer)|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Masaru (writer)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, *Illustrated Guide to Shindai Characters: Read, Write, and Use* (図説神代文字入門-読める書ける使える), Being Net Press, 2007. ISBN 978-4-434-10165-6 * {{ill|Ōtsuka Hatsushige|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsuka Hatsushige|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kobayashi Saburō (archaeologist)|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Saburō (archaeologist)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Kumano Masaya (eds.), *Nihon Kofun Daijiten* (日本古墳大辞典), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1989. ISBN 978-4-490-10260-4 * {{ill|Shiomi Keiji|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiomi Keiji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, *The Mystery of Decorated Kofun* (装飾古墳のふしぎ), Ashi Shobō. ISBN 978-4-751-20117-6 * Ukiha City Board of Education, *Asada Kofun Group*. == See Also == * {{ill|Decorated kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Decorated kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Temiya Cave inscriptions|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temiya Cave inscriptions|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Banpo script|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banpo script|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Coastal Zenpokoenfun|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Coastal Zenpokoenfun|qq=}}
* {{ill|Sōhō-chūen Kofun|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōhō-chūen Kofun|qq=}} * == External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20160304185652/http://www.city.ukiha.fukuoka.jp/imgkiji/pub/detail.aspx?c_id=71&id=2&pg=3 Kofun] (History & Traditional Culture) - Ukiha City (Archived)

== Shigesada Kofun == [[File:Shigesada Kofun.jpg|thumb|Information board explaining the Shigesada Kofun. The top left shows a quiver (yuki), while the bottom left shows concentric circles.]] {{ill|Shigesada Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigesada Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} located on the left bank of the {{ill|Chikugo River|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo River|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is part of the {{ill|Asada Kofun Group|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asada Kofun Group|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and its construction is dated to the latter half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/6th century|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. On March 8, 1922 ({{ill|Taishō era|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō era|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 11), it was designated a {{ill|National Historic Site (Japan)|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Historic Site (Japan)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} along with the nearby Kusuna Kofun.

In the rear chamber, quivers and a few concentric circles dominate, while the front chamber primarily features concentric circles, indicating a distinct pattern distribution between the two sections. These murals, composed of quivers and concentric circles, are what were interpreted as "Chikushi Characters."

Decorated kofun of this type are concentrated in northern Kyushu.
== References == * {{ill|Ochiai Naozumi|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochiai Naozumi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, *Nihon Kodai Moji Kō* (日本古代文字考), Yoshikawa Hanshichi, Komakisha, 1888. {{NDL|53013099}} * {{ill|Ago Kiyohiko|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ago Kiyohiko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, *Nihon Shindai Moji: Kodai Waji Sōkan* (日本神代文字-古代和字総観), Tairiku Shobō, 1975. * Ago Kiyohiko, *Nihon Shindai Moji Kenkyū Genten* (日本神代文字研究原典), Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha, 1996. <!--Tsukushi script--><!--Tsukushi script--><!--Tsukushi script--><!--Tsukushi script--> [[Category:Kofun (general concept)]] [[Category:Jindai moji]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11602924}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:筑紫文字]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11602924]] Wikidata name: Tsukushi script [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Chikushi Moji.jpg|thumb|Illustration of the mural in the Shigesada Kofun]] {{ill|Tsukushi script|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukushi script|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (つくしもじ) are a form of [[:en:Jindai moji|Jindai moji]] said to be inscribed on the stone chamber of {{ill|Shigesada Kofun|ja|重定古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigesada Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ukiha, Fukuoka|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukiha, Fukuoka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Japan. They are also referred to as Shigesada Cave Ancient Characters (Shigesada Sekkutsu Koji, '''重定石窟古字''').
== Overview == {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirata Atsutane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} recorded these characters in his 1819 ({{ill|Bunsei|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunsei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 2) book *{{ill|Shinji Hinomibumi-den|ja|神字日文伝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinji Hinomibumi-den|1=Shinji Hinomibumi-den|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589447}}* (神字日文伝), listing them as "Stone Cave Characters of Chikugo Province." According to Hirata, there was a local tradition that referred to them as "characters from the age of the gods." However, he noted, *"It is difficult to ascertain whether they are truly characters."* The drawings of these inscriptions published in his work were originally obtained from a scholar of the {{ill|Kurume Domain|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurume Domain|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, passed on to Hirata through the Kokugaku scholar {{ill|Yashiro Hirokata|ja|屋代弘賢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashiro Hirokata|1=Yashiro Hirokata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6538130}}.
Due to Hirata's influence, interest in the inscriptions grew. When {{ill|Inō Tadataka|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inō Tadataka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} visited Chikugo Province for surveying, he also investigated the Shigesada Kofun, conducting a detailed examination. This research, along with that of the Kurume Domain scholars, remains a valuable resource due to the scarcity of early documentation on decorated kofun.
On April 22, 1825 (Bunsei 8, 3rd month, 5th day), Kurume Domain retainers Murakami Yasutoshi and Hayakawa Issei created a detailed copy of the inscriptions. This illustration was later included in the work *{{ill|Nihon Kodai Moji Kō|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kodai Moji Kō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}* by the Kokugaku scholar {{ill|Ochiai Naozumi|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochiai Naozumi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Ochiai theorized that the script was formed by combining four types of symbols with eleven additional symbols. He also noted that some dot-like marks resembled the diacritic dots used in {{ill|Siddhaṃ script|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Siddhaṃ script|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, used for indicating long vowels. Additionally, historian {{ill|Kamiya Yoshimichi|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Yoshimichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} pointed out in his 1886 (Meiji 19) article *Kodaiji Kō* (*古代文字考*), published in the *Journal of the Tokyo Anthropological Society*, that similar inscriptions were found on a stone from the residence of {{ill|Matsuura Takeshirō|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuura Takeshirō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (formerly part of a castle wall), as well as on the {{ill|Temple bell|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple bell|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of {{ill|Takase Shrine|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takase Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Tonami District, Etchū Province (modern-day {{ill|Nanto, Toyama|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto, Toyama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
The *{{ill|Takenouchi Documents|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Documents|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}* mention these inscriptions as "Yasoyo Characters" (ヤソヨ文字). According to *Shindai no Bankokushi* (神代の万国史) by {{ill|Takenouchi Yoshimiya|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Yoshimiya|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, these characters were created during the reign of the 22nd emperor of the Ninth Ancient Dynasty. *Nippon Ji no Hakkutsu* (につぽん字の発掘) by {{ill|Sakai Yoshio|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Yoshio|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} claims they originated in the era of Emperor *Amanohihirumotoashikabikikami*, the first emperor of humanity. (For more information on the emperors mentioned in the Takenouchi Documents, see {{ill|Takenouchi Documents' List of Emperors|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Documents' List of Emperors|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.)
Modern research considers these "Chikushi Characters" to be murals rather than syllabary-based writing, similar to the case of the so-called {{ill|Temiya Cave inscriptions|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temiya Cave inscriptions|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hokkaido. {{ill|Hara Masaru|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Masaru|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in *Illustrated Guide to Shindai Characters* (*図説神代文字入門*), states: *"The attempts of Hirata and Ochiai were, in a way, akin to modern archaeology, as they sought to decipher messages left by ancient people."*
== Shigesada Kofun == [[File:Shigesada Kofun.jpg|thumb|Information board explaining the Shigesada Kofun. The top left shows a quiver (yuki), while the bottom left shows concentric circles.]] {{ill|Shigesada Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigesada Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} located on the left bank of the {{ill|Chikugo River|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is part of the {{ill|Asada Kofun Group|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asada Kofun Group|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and its construction is dated to the latter half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/6th century|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. On March 8, 1922 ({{ill|Taishō era|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 11), it was designated a {{ill|National Historic Site (Japan)|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Historic Site (Japan)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} along with the nearby Kusuna Kofun.
The front part of the mound has been lost, so its current length is 51 meters, but it is estimated to have originally been around 80 meters. The rear circular section has a diameter of 44 meters and a height of 8.5 meters. The rear section contains a stone chamber measuring 17 meters in length and 3.8 meters in height, connected to the exterior by a 6-meter-long passage. The chamber is notable for its use of massive andesite stones, with single large slabs forming the back wall, side walls, ceiling, and stone shelves.
The chamber walls are decorated with red and green pigments depicting {{ill|quivers (yuki)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/quivers (yuki)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (containers for carrying arrows), concentric circles, and fern-like motifs. In the rear chamber, quivers and a few concentric circles dominate, while the front chamber primarily features concentric circles, indicating a distinct pattern distribution between the two sections. These murals, composed of quivers and concentric circles, are what were interpreted as "Chikushi Characters."
The inscriptions and murals have been damaged due to various factors. During World War II, the tomb was used as an air raid shelter, and in the postwar period, a surge of interest in archaeology led to increased foot traffic, further eroding the markings. In 1962 (Shōwa 37), Tokyo University of the Arts professor Kusaka Hakkō created a restoration drawing (partially reconstructed). Copies of this restoration are preserved at the Ukiha City Museum of History and Folklore, and an enlarged version is displayed at the entrance of the Ukiha City Library.
Local folklore states that numerous earthenware vessels and ironware were once stored in the tomb. It is said that the local people would borrow these objects when needed, but when greedy individuals failed to return them, they disappeared.
Another kofun in Fukuoka Prefecture, the {{ill|Ōzuka Kofun (Katsuragawa, Fukuoka)|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōzuka Kofun (Katsuragawa, Fukuoka)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Katsuragawa, Fukuoka|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuragawa, Fukuoka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, features murals similar to those of the Shigesada Kofun, including quivers and concentric circles. Decorated kofun of this type are concentrated in northern Kyushu.

== Shigesada Kofun == [[File:Shigesada Kofun.jpg|thumb|Information board explaining the Shigesada Kofun. The top left shows a quiver (yuki), while the bottom left shows concentric circles.]] {{ill|Shigesada Kofun|ja|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigesada Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} located on the left bank of the {{ill|Chikugo River|ja|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Another kofun in Fukuoka Prefecture, the {{ill|Ōzuka Kofun (Katsuragawa, Fukuoka)|ja|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōzuka Kofun (Katsuragawa, Fukuoka)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Katsuragawa, Fukuoka|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuragawa, Fukuoka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, features murals similar to those of the Shigesada Kofun, including quivers and concentric circles. Decorated kofun of this type are concentrated in northern Kyushu.

ISBN 978-4-404-02328-5 * {{ill|Hara Masaru (writer)|ja|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Masaru (writer)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, *Illustrated Guide to Shindai Characters: Read, Write, and Use* (図説神代文字入門-読める書ける使える), Being Net Press, 2007. ISBN 978-4-434-10165-6 * {{ill|Ōtsuka Hatsushige|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsuka Hatsushige|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kobayashi Saburō (archaeologist)|ja|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Saburō (archaeologist)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Kumano Masaya (eds.), *Nihon Kofun Daijiten* (日本古墳大辞典), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1989. ISBN 978-4-490-10260-4 * {{ill|Shiomi Keiji|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiomi Keiji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, *The Mystery of Decorated Kofun* (装飾古墳のふしぎ), Ashi Shobō. <!--Tsukushi script--><!--Tsukushi script--><!--Tsukushi script--><!--Tsukushi script--> [[Category:Kofun (general concept)]] [[Category:Jindai moji]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11602924}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:筑紫文字]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11602924]] Wikidata name: Tsukushi script [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Chikushi Moji.jpg|thumb|Illustration of the mural in the Shigesada Kofun]] {{ill|Tsukushi script|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukushi script|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (つくしもじ) are a form of [[:en:Jindai moji|Jindai moji]] said to be inscribed on the stone chamber of {{ill|Shigesada Kofun|ja|重定古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigesada Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ukiha, Fukuoka|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukiha, Fukuoka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Japan. They are also referred to as Shigesada Cave Ancient Characters (Shigesada Sekkutsu Koji, '''重定石窟古字''').
== Overview == {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirata Atsutane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} recorded these characters in his 1819 ({{ill|Bunsei|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunsei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 2) book *{{ill|Shinji Hinomibumi-den|ja|神字日文伝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinji Hinomibumi-den|1=Shinji Hinomibumi-den|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589447}}* (神字日文伝), listing them as "Stone Cave Characters of Chikugo Province." According to Hirata, there was a local tradition that referred to them as "characters from the age of the gods." However, he noted, *"It is difficult to ascertain whether they are truly characters."* The drawings of these inscriptions published in his work were originally obtained from a scholar of the {{ill|Kurume Domain|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurume Domain|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, passed on to Hirata through the Kokugaku scholar {{ill|Yashiro Hirokata|ja|屋代弘賢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashiro Hirokata|1=Yashiro Hirokata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6538130}}.
Due to Hirata's influence, interest in the inscriptions grew. When {{ill|Inō Tadataka|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inō Tadataka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} visited Chikugo Province for surveying, he also investigated the Shigesada Kofun, conducting a detailed examination. This research, along with that of the Kurume Domain scholars, remains a valuable resource due to the scarcity of early documentation on decorated kofun.
On April 22, 1825 (Bunsei 8, 3rd month, 5th day), Kurume Domain retainers Murakami Yasutoshi and Hayakawa Issei created a detailed copy of the inscriptions. This illustration was later included in the work *{{ill|Nihon Kodai Moji Kō|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kodai Moji Kō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}* by the Kokugaku scholar {{ill|Ochiai Naozumi|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochiai Naozumi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Ochiai theorized that the script was formed by combining four types of symbols with eleven additional symbols. He also noted that some dot-like marks resembled the diacritic dots used in {{ill|Siddhaṃ script|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Siddhaṃ script|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, used for indicating long vowels. Additionally, historian {{ill|Kamiya Yoshimichi|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Yoshimichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} pointed out in his 1886 (Meiji 19) article *Kodaiji Kō* (*古代文字考*), published in the *Journal of the Tokyo Anthropological Society*, that similar inscriptions were found on a stone from the residence of {{ill|Matsuura Takeshirō|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuura Takeshirō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (formerly part of a castle wall), as well as on the {{ill|Temple bell|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple bell|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of {{ill|Takase Shrine|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takase Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Tonami District, Etchū Province (modern-day {{ill|Nanto, Toyama|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto, Toyama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
The *{{ill|Takenouchi Documents|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Documents|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}* mention these inscriptions as "Yasoyo Characters" (ヤソヨ文字). According to *Shindai no Bankokushi* (神代の万国史) by {{ill|Takenouchi Yoshimiya|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Yoshimiya|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, these characters were created during the reign of the 22nd emperor of the Ninth Ancient Dynasty. *Nippon Ji no Hakkutsu* (につぽん字の発掘) by {{ill|Sakai Yoshio|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Yoshio|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} claims they originated in the era of Emperor *Amanohihirumotoashikabikikami*, the first emperor of humanity. (For more information on the emperors mentioned in the Takenouchi Documents, see {{ill|Takenouchi Documents' List of Emperors|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Documents' List of Emperors|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.)
Modern research considers these "Chikushi Characters" to be murals rather than syllabary-based writing, similar to the case of the so-called {{ill|Temiya Cave inscriptions|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temiya Cave inscriptions|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hokkaido. {{ill|Hara Masaru|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Masaru|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in *Illustrated Guide to Shindai Characters* (*図説神代文字入門*), states: *"The attempts of Hirata and Ochiai were, in a way, akin to modern archaeology, as they sought to decipher messages left by ancient people."*
== Shigesada Kofun == [[File:Shigesada Kofun.jpg|thumb|Information board explaining the Shigesada Kofun. The top left shows a quiver (yuki), while the bottom left shows concentric circles.]] {{ill|Shigesada Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigesada Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} located on the left bank of the {{ill|Chikugo River|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is part of the {{ill|Asada Kofun Group|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asada Kofun Group|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and its construction is dated to the latter half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/6th century|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. On March 8, 1922 ({{ill|Taishō era|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 11), it was designated a {{ill|National Historic Site (Japan)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Historic Site (Japan)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} along with the nearby Kusuna Kofun.
The front part of the mound has been lost, so its current length is 51 meters, but it is estimated to have originally been around 80 meters. The rear circular section has a diameter of 44 meters and a height of 8.5 meters. The rear section contains a stone chamber measuring 17 meters in length and 3.8 meters in height, connected to the exterior by a 6-meter-long passage. The chamber is notable for its use of massive andesite stones, with single large slabs forming the back wall, side walls, ceiling, and stone shelves.
The chamber walls are decorated with red and green pigments depicting {{ill|quivers (yuki)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/quivers (yuki)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (containers for carrying arrows), concentric circles, and fern-like motifs. In the rear chamber, quivers and a few concentric circles dominate, while the front chamber primarily features concentric circles, indicating a distinct pattern distribution between the two sections. These murals, composed of quivers and concentric circles, are what were interpreted as "Chikushi Characters."
The inscriptions and murals have been damaged due to various factors. During World War II, the tomb was used as an air raid shelter, and in the postwar period, a surge of interest in archaeology led to increased foot traffic, further eroding the markings. In 1962 (Shōwa 37), Tokyo University of the Arts professor Kusaka Hakkō created a restoration drawing (partially reconstructed). Copies of this restoration are preserved at the Ukiha City Museum of History and Folklore, and an enlarged version is displayed at the entrance of the Ukiha City Library.
Local folklore states that numerous earthenware vessels and ironware were once stored in the tomb. It is said that the local people would borrow these objects when needed, but when greedy individuals failed to return them, they disappeared.
Another kofun in Fukuoka Prefecture, the {{ill|Ōzuka Kofun (Katsuragawa, Fukuoka)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōzuka Kofun (Katsuragawa, Fukuoka)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Katsuragawa, Fukuoka|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuragawa, Fukuoka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, features murals similar to those of the Shigesada Kofun, including quivers and concentric circles. Decorated kofun of this type are concentrated in northern Kyushu.
== References == * {{ill|Ochiai Naozumi|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochiai Naozumi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, *Nihon Kodai Moji Kō* (日本古代文字考), Yoshikawa Hanshichi, Komakisha, 1888. {{NDL|53013099}} * {{ill|Ago Kiyohiko|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ago Kiyohiko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, *Nihon Shindai Moji: Kodai Waji Sōkan* (日本神代文字-古代和字総観), Tairiku Shobō, 1975. * Ago Kiyohiko, *Nihon Shindai Moji Kenkyū Genten* (日本神代文字研究原典), Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-404-02328-5 * {{ill|Hara Masaru (writer)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Masaru (writer)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, *Illustrated Guide to Shindai Characters: Read, Write, and Use* (図説神代文字入門-読める書ける使える), Being Net Press, 2007. ISBN 978-4-434-10165-6 * {{ill|Ōtsuka Hatsushige|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsuka Hatsushige|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kobayashi Saburō (archaeologist)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Saburō (archaeologist)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Kumano Masaya (eds.), *Nihon Kofun Daijiten* (日本古墳大辞典), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1989. ISBN 978-4-490-10260-4 * {{ill|Shiomi Keiji|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiomi Keiji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, *The Mystery of Decorated Kofun* (装飾古墳のふしぎ), Ashi Shobō. ISBN 978-4-751-20117-6 * Ukiha City Board of Education, *Asada Kofun Group*. == See Also == * {{ill|Decorated kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Decorated kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Temiya Cave inscriptions|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temiya Cave inscriptions|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Banpo script|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banpo script|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

== Shigesada Kofun == [[File:Shigesada Kofun.jpg|thumb|Information board explaining the Shigesada Kofun. The top left shows a quiver (yuki), while the bottom left shows concentric circles.]] {{ill|Shigesada Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigesada Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} located on the left bank of the {{ill|Chikugo River|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is part of the {{ill|Asada Kofun Group|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asada Kofun Group|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and its construction is dated to the latter half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/6th century|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. On March 8, 1922 ({{ill|Taishō era|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 11), it was designated a {{ill|National Historic Site (Japan)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Historic Site (Japan)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} along with the nearby Kusuna Kofun.
The front part of the mound has been lost, so its current length is 51 meters, but it is estimated to have originally been around 80 meters. The rear circular section has a diameter of 44 meters and a height of 8.5 meters. The rear section contains a stone chamber measuring 17 meters in length and 3.8 meters in height, connected to the exterior by a 6-meter-long passage. The chamber is notable for its use of massive andesite stones, with single large slabs forming the back wall, side walls, ceiling, and stone shelves.
The chamber walls are decorated with red and green pigments depicting {{ill|quivers (yuki)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/quivers (yuki)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (containers for carrying arrows), concentric circles, and fern-like motifs. In the rear chamber, quivers and a few concentric circles dominate, while the front chamber primarily features concentric circles, indicating a distinct pattern distribution between the two sections. These murals, composed of quivers and concentric circles, are what were interpreted as "Chikushi Characters."
The inscriptions and murals have been damaged due to various factors. During World War II, the tomb was used as an air raid shelter, and in the postwar period, a surge of interest in archaeology led to increased foot traffic, further eroding the markings. In 1962 (Shōwa 37), Tokyo University of the Arts professor Kusaka Hakkō created a restoration drawing (partially reconstructed). Copies of this restoration are preserved at the Ukiha City Museum of History and Folklore, and an enlarged version is displayed at the entrance of the Ukiha City Library.
Local folklore states that numerous earthenware vessels and ironware were once stored in the tomb. It is said that the local people would borrow these objects when needed, but when greedy individuals failed to return them, they disappeared.
Another kofun in Fukuoka Prefecture, the {{ill|Ōzuka Kofun (Katsuragawa, Fukuoka)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōzuka Kofun (Katsuragawa, Fukuoka)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Katsuragawa, Fukuoka|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuragawa, Fukuoka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, features murals similar to those of the Shigesada Kofun, including quivers and concentric circles. Decorated kofun of this type are concentrated in northern Kyushu.
== References == * {{ill|Ochiai Naozumi|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochiai Naozumi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, *Nihon Kodai Moji Kō* (日本古代文字考), Yoshikawa Hanshichi, Komakisha, 1888. {{NDL|53013099}} * {{ill|Ago Kiyohiko|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ago Kiyohiko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, *Nihon Shindai Moji: Kodai Waji Sōkan* (日本神代文字-古代和字総観), Tairiku Shobō, 1975. * Ago Kiyohiko, *Nihon Shindai Moji Kenkyū Genten* (日本神代文字研究原典), Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-404-02328-5 * {{ill|Hara Masaru (writer)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Masaru (writer)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, *Illustrated Guide to Shindai Characters: Read, Write, and Use* (図説神代文字入門-読める書ける使える), Being Net Press, 2007. ISBN 978-4-434-10165-6 * {{ill|Ōtsuka Hatsushige|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsuka Hatsushige|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kobayashi Saburō (archaeologist)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Saburō (archaeologist)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Kumano Masaya (eds.), *Nihon Kofun Daijiten* (日本古墳大辞典), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1989. ISBN 978-4-490-10260-4 * {{ill|Shiomi Keiji|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiomi Keiji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, *The Mystery of Decorated Kofun* (装飾古墳のふしぎ), Ashi Shobō. ISBN 978-4-751-20117-6 * Ukiha City Board of Education, *Asada Kofun Group*. == See Also == * {{ill|Decorated kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Decorated kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Temiya Cave inscriptions|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temiya Cave inscriptions|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Banpo script|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banpo script|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

<!--Tsukushi script--><!--Tsukushi script--><!--Tsukushi script--><!--Tsukushi script--> [[Category:Kofun (general concept)]] [[Category:Jindai moji]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11602924}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.comment: Bot: Add Wikidata has English label - based on Wikidata English label
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:筑紫文字]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11602924]] Wikidata name: Tsukushi script [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Chikushi Moji.jpg|thumb|Illustration of the mural in the Shigesada Kofun]] {{ill|Tsukushi script|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukushi script|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (つくしもじ) are a form of [[:en:Jindai moji|Jindai moji]] said to be inscribed on the stone chamber of {{ill|Shigesada Kofun|ja|重定古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigesada Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ukiha, Fukuoka|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukiha, Fukuoka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Japan. They are also referred to as Shigesada Cave Ancient Characters (Shigesada Sekkutsu Koji, '''重定石窟古字''').
== Overview == {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirata Atsutane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} recorded these characters in his 1819 ({{ill|Bunsei|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunsei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 2) book *{{ill|Shinji Hinomibumi-den|ja|神字日文伝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinji Hinomibumi-den|1=Shinji Hinomibumi-den|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589447}}* (神字日文伝), listing them as "Stone Cave Characters of Chikugo Province." According to Hirata, there was a local tradition that referred to them as "characters from the age of the gods." However, he noted, *"It is difficult to ascertain whether they are truly characters."* The drawings of these inscriptions published in his work were originally obtained from a scholar of the {{ill|Kurume Domain|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurume Domain|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, passed on to Hirata through the Kokugaku scholar {{ill|Yashiro Hirokata|ja|屋代弘賢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashiro Hirokata|1=Yashiro Hirokata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6538130}}.
Due to Hirata's influence, interest in the inscriptions grew. When {{ill|Inō Tadataka|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inō Tadataka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} visited Chikugo Province for surveying, he also investigated the Shigesada Kofun, conducting a detailed examination. This research, along with that of the Kurume Domain scholars, remains a valuable resource due to the scarcity of early documentation on decorated kofun.
On April 22, 1825 (Bunsei 8, 3rd month, 5th day), Kurume Domain retainers Murakami Yasutoshi and Hayakawa Issei created a detailed copy of the inscriptions. This illustration was later included in the work *{{ill|Nihon Kodai Moji Kō|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kodai Moji Kō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}* by the Kokugaku scholar {{ill|Ochiai Naozumi|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochiai Naozumi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Ochiai theorized that the script was formed by combining four types of symbols with eleven additional symbols. He also noted that some dot-like marks resembled the diacritic dots used in {{ill|Siddhaṃ script|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Siddhaṃ script|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, used for indicating long vowels. Additionally, historian {{ill|Kamiya Yoshimichi|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Yoshimichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} pointed out in his 1886 (Meiji 19) article *Kodaiji Kō* (*古代文字考*), published in the *Journal of the Tokyo Anthropological Society*, that similar inscriptions were found on a stone from the residence of {{ill|Matsuura Takeshirō|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuura Takeshirō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (formerly part of a castle wall), as well as on the {{ill|Temple bell|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple bell|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of {{ill|Takase Shrine|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takase Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Tonami District, Etchū Province (modern-day {{ill|Nanto, Toyama|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto, Toyama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
The *{{ill|Takenouchi Documents|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Documents|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}* mention these inscriptions as "Yasoyo Characters" (ヤソヨ文字). According to *Shindai no Bankokushi* (神代の万国史) by {{ill|Takenouchi Yoshimiya|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Yoshimiya|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, these characters were created during the reign of the 22nd emperor of the Ninth Ancient Dynasty. *Nippon Ji no Hakkutsu* (につぽん字の発掘) by {{ill|Sakai Yoshio|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Yoshio|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} claims they originated in the era of Emperor *Amanohihirumotoashikabikikami*, the first emperor of humanity. (For more information on the emperors mentioned in the Takenouchi Documents, see {{ill|Takenouchi Documents' List of Emperors|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Documents' List of Emperors|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.)
Modern research considers these "Chikushi Characters" to be murals rather than syllabary-based writing, similar to the case of the so-called {{ill|Temiya Cave inscriptions|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temiya Cave inscriptions|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hokkaido. {{ill|Hara Masaru|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Masaru|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in *Illustrated Guide to Shindai Characters* (*図説神代文字入門*), states: *"The attempts of Hirata and Ochiai were, in a way, akin to modern archaeology, as they sought to decipher messages left by ancient people."*
== Shigesada Kofun == [[File:Shigesada Kofun.jpg|thumb|Information board explaining the Shigesada Kofun. The top left shows a quiver (yuki), while the bottom left shows concentric circles.]] {{ill|Shigesada Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigesada Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} located on the left bank of the {{ill|Chikugo River|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is part of the {{ill|Asada Kofun Group|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asada Kofun Group|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and its construction is dated to the latter half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/6th century|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. On March 8, 1922 ({{ill|Taishō era|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 11), it was designated a {{ill|National Historic Site (Japan)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Historic Site (Japan)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} along with the nearby Kusuna Kofun.
The front part of the mound has been lost, so its current length is 51 meters, but it is estimated to have originally been around 80 meters. The rear circular section has a diameter of 44 meters and a height of 8.5 meters. The rear section contains a stone chamber measuring 17 meters in length and 3.8 meters in height, connected to the exterior by a 6-meter-long passage. The chamber is notable for its use of massive andesite stones, with single large slabs forming the back wall, side walls, ceiling, and stone shelves.
The chamber walls are decorated with red and green pigments depicting {{ill|quivers (yuki)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/quivers (yuki)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (containers for carrying arrows), concentric circles, and fern-like motifs. In the rear chamber, quivers and a few concentric circles dominate, while the front chamber primarily features concentric circles, indicating a distinct pattern distribution between the two sections. These murals, composed of quivers and concentric circles, are what were interpreted as "Chikushi Characters."
The inscriptions and murals have been damaged due to various factors. During World War II, the tomb was used as an air raid shelter, and in the postwar period, a surge of interest in archaeology led to increased foot traffic, further eroding the markings. In 1962 (Shōwa 37), Tokyo University of the Arts professor Kusaka Hakkō created a restoration drawing (partially reconstructed). Copies of this restoration are preserved at the Ukiha City Museum of History and Folklore, and an enlarged version is displayed at the entrance of the Ukiha City Library.
Local folklore states that numerous earthenware vessels and ironware were once stored in the tomb. It is said that the local people would borrow these objects when needed, but when greedy individuals failed to return them, they disappeared.
Another kofun in Fukuoka Prefecture, the {{ill|Ōzuka Kofun (Katsuragawa, Fukuoka)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōzuka Kofun (Katsuragawa, Fukuoka)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Katsuragawa, Fukuoka|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuragawa, Fukuoka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, features murals similar to those of the Shigesada Kofun, including quivers and concentric circles. Decorated kofun of this type are concentrated in northern Kyushu.
== References == * {{ill|Ochiai Naozumi|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochiai Naozumi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, *Nihon Kodai Moji Kō* (日本古代文字考), Yoshikawa Hanshichi, Komakisha, 1888. {{NDL|53013099}} * {{ill|Ago Kiyohiko|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ago Kiyohiko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, *Nihon Shindai Moji: Kodai Waji Sōkan* (日本神代文字-古代和字総観), Tairiku Shobō, 1975. * Ago Kiyohiko, *Nihon Shindai Moji Kenkyū Genten* (日本神代文字研究原典), Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-404-02328-5 * {{ill|Hara Masaru (writer)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Masaru (writer)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, *Illustrated Guide to Shindai Characters: Read, Write, and Use* (図説神代文字入門-読める書ける使える), Being Net Press, 2007. ISBN 978-4-434-10165-6 * {{ill|Ōtsuka Hatsushige|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsuka Hatsushige|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kobayashi Saburō (archaeologist)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Saburō (archaeologist)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Kumano Masaya (eds.), *Nihon Kofun Daijiten* (日本古墳大辞典), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1989. ISBN 978-4-490-10260-4 * {{ill|Shiomi Keiji|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiomi Keiji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, *The Mystery of Decorated Kofun* (装飾古墳のふしぎ), Ashi Shobō. ISBN 978-4-751-20117-6 * Ukiha City Board of Education, *Asada Kofun Group*. == See Also == * {{ill|Decorated kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Decorated kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Temiya Cave inscriptions|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temiya Cave inscriptions|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Banpo script|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banpo script|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Coastal Zenpokoenfun|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Coastal Zenpokoenfun|qq=}}
* {{ill|Sōhō-chūen Kofun|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōhō-chūen Kofun|qq=}} * == External Links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20160304185652/http://www.city.ukiha.fukuoka.jp/imgkiji/pub/detail.aspx?c_id=71&id=2&pg=3 Kofun] (History & Traditional Culture) - Ukiha City (Archived)

== Shigesada Kofun == [[File:Shigesada Kofun.jpg|thumb|Information board explaining the Shigesada Kofun. The top left shows a quiver (yuki), while the bottom left shows concentric circles.]] {{ill|Shigesada Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigesada Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} located on the left bank of the {{ill|Chikugo River|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is part of the {{ill|Asada Kofun Group|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asada Kofun Group|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and its construction is dated to the latter half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/6th century|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. On March 8, 1922 ({{ill|Taishō era|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 11), it was designated a {{ill|National Historic Site (Japan)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Historic Site (Japan)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} along with the nearby Kusuna Kofun.
The front part of the mound has been lost, so its current length is 51 meters, but it is estimated to have originally been around 80 meters. The rear circular section has a diameter of 44 meters and a height of 8.5 meters. The rear section contains a stone chamber measuring 17 meters in length and 3.8 meters in height, connected to the exterior by a 6-meter-long passage. The chamber is notable for its use of massive andesite stones, with single large slabs forming the back wall, side walls, ceiling, and stone shelves.
The chamber walls are decorated with red and green pigments depicting {{ill|quivers (yuki)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/quivers (yuki)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (containers for carrying arrows), concentric circles, and fern-like motifs. In the rear chamber, quivers and a few concentric circles dominate, while the front chamber primarily features concentric circles, indicating a distinct pattern distribution between the two sections. These murals, composed of quivers and concentric circles, are what were interpreted as "Chikushi Characters."
The inscriptions and murals have been damaged due to various factors. During World War II, the tomb was used as an air raid shelter, and in the postwar period, a surge of interest in archaeology led to increased foot traffic, further eroding the markings. In 1962 (Shōwa 37), Tokyo University of the Arts professor Kusaka Hakkō created a restoration drawing (partially reconstructed). Copies of this restoration are preserved at the Ukiha City Museum of History and Folklore, and an enlarged version is displayed at the entrance of the Ukiha City Library.
Local folklore states that numerous earthenware vessels and ironware were once stored in the tomb. It is said that the local people would borrow these objects when needed, but when greedy individuals failed to return them, they disappeared.
Another kofun in Fukuoka Prefecture, the {{ill|Ōzuka Kofun (Katsuragawa, Fukuoka)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōzuka Kofun (Katsuragawa, Fukuoka)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Katsuragawa, Fukuoka|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuragawa, Fukuoka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, features murals similar to those of the Shigesada Kofun, including quivers and concentric circles. Decorated kofun of this type are concentrated in northern Kyushu.

<!--Tsukushi script--><!--Tsukushi script--><!--Tsukushi script--><!--Tsukushi script--> [[Category:Kofun (general concept)]] [[Category:Jindai moji]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11602924}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:筑紫文字]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11602924]] Wikidata name: Tsukushi script [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Chikushi Moji.jpg|thumb|Illustration of the mural in the Shigesada Kofun]] {{ill|Tsukushi script|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukushi script|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (つくしもじ) are a form of [[:en:Jindai moji|Jindai moji]] said to be inscribed on the stone chamber of {{ill|Shigesada Kofun|ja|重定古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigesada Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ukiha, Fukuoka|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukiha, Fukuoka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Japan. They are also referred to as Shigesada Cave Ancient Characters (Shigesada Sekkutsu Koji, '''重定石窟古字''').
== Overview == {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirata Atsutane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} recorded these characters in his 1819 ({{ill|Bunsei|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunsei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 2) book *{{ill|Shinji Hinomibumi-den|ja|神字日文伝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinji Hinomibumi-den|1=Shinji Hinomibumi-den|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589447}}* (神字日文伝), listing them as "Stone Cave Characters of Chikugo Province." According to Hirata, there was a local tradition that referred to them as "characters from the age of the gods." However, he noted, *"It is difficult to ascertain whether they are truly characters."* The drawings of these inscriptions published in his work were originally obtained from a scholar of the {{ill|Kurume Domain|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurume Domain|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, passed on to Hirata through the Kokugaku scholar {{ill|Yashiro Hirokata|ja|屋代弘賢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashiro Hirokata|1=Yashiro Hirokata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6538130}}.
Due to Hirata's influence, interest in the inscriptions grew. When {{ill|Inō Tadataka|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inō Tadataka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} visited Chikugo Province for surveying, he also investigated the Shigesada Kofun, conducting a detailed examination. This research, along with that of the Kurume Domain scholars, remains a valuable resource due to the scarcity of early documentation on decorated kofun.
On April 22, 1825 (Bunsei 8, 3rd month, 5th day), Kurume Domain retainers Murakami Yasutoshi and Hayakawa Issei created a detailed copy of the inscriptions. This illustration was later included in the work *{{ill|Nihon Kodai Moji Kō|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kodai Moji Kō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}* by the Kokugaku scholar {{ill|Ochiai Naozumi|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochiai Naozumi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Ochiai theorized that the script was formed by combining four types of symbols with eleven additional symbols. He also noted that some dot-like marks resembled the diacritic dots used in {{ill|Siddhaṃ script|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Siddhaṃ script|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, used for indicating long vowels. Additionally, historian {{ill|Kamiya Yoshimichi|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Yoshimichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} pointed out in his 1886 (Meiji 19) article *Kodaiji Kō* (*古代文字考*), published in the *Journal of the Tokyo Anthropological Society*, that similar inscriptions were found on a stone from the residence of {{ill|Matsuura Takeshirō|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuura Takeshirō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (formerly part of a castle wall), as well as on the {{ill|Temple bell|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple bell|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of {{ill|Takase Shrine|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takase Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Tonami District, Etchū Province (modern-day {{ill|Nanto, Toyama|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto, Toyama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
The *{{ill|Takenouchi Documents|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Documents|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}* mention these inscriptions as "Yasoyo Characters" (ヤソヨ文字). According to *Shindai no Bankokushi* (神代の万国史) by {{ill|Takenouchi Yoshimiya|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Yoshimiya|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, these characters were created during the reign of the 22nd emperor of the Ninth Ancient Dynasty. *Nippon Ji no Hakkutsu* (につぽん字の発掘) by {{ill|Sakai Yoshio|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Yoshio|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} claims they originated in the era of Emperor *Amanohihirumotoashikabikikami*, the first emperor of humanity. (For more information on the emperors mentioned in the Takenouchi Documents, see {{ill|Takenouchi Documents' List of Emperors|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Documents' List of Emperors|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.)
Modern research considers these "Chikushi Characters" to be murals rather than syllabary-based writing, similar to the case of the so-called {{ill|Temiya Cave inscriptions|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temiya Cave inscriptions|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hokkaido. {{ill|Hara Masaru|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Masaru|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in *Illustrated Guide to Shindai Characters* (*図説神代文字入門*), states: *"The attempts of Hirata and Ochiai were, in a way, akin to modern archaeology, as they sought to decipher messages left by ancient people."*
== Shigesada Kofun == [[File:Shigesada Kofun.jpg|thumb|Information board explaining the Shigesada Kofun. The top left shows a quiver (yuki), while the bottom left shows concentric circles.]] {{ill|Shigesada Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigesada Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} located on the left bank of the {{ill|Chikugo River|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is part of the {{ill|Asada Kofun Group|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asada Kofun Group|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and its construction is dated to the latter half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/6th century|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. On March 8, 1922 ({{ill|Taishō era|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 11), it was designated a {{ill|National Historic Site (Japan)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Historic Site (Japan)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} along with the nearby Kusuna Kofun.
The front part of the mound has been lost, so its current length is 51 meters, but it is estimated to have originally been around 80 meters. The rear circular section has a diameter of 44 meters and a height of 8.5 meters. The rear section contains a stone chamber measuring 17 meters in length and 3.8 meters in height, connected to the exterior by a 6-meter-long passage. The chamber is notable for its use of massive andesite stones, with single large slabs forming the back wall, side walls, ceiling, and stone shelves.
The chamber walls are decorated with red and green pigments depicting {{ill|quivers (yuki)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/quivers (yuki)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (containers for carrying arrows), concentric circles, and fern-like motifs. In the rear chamber, quivers and a few concentric circles dominate, while the front chamber primarily features concentric circles, indicating a distinct pattern distribution between the two sections. These murals, composed of quivers and concentric circles, are what were interpreted as "Chikushi Characters."
The inscriptions and murals have been damaged due to various factors. During World War II, the tomb was used as an air raid shelter, and in the postwar period, a surge of interest in archaeology led to increased foot traffic, further eroding the markings. In 1962 (Shōwa 37), Tokyo University of the Arts professor Kusaka Hakkō created a restoration drawing (partially reconstructed). Copies of this restoration are preserved at the Ukiha City Museum of History and Folklore, and an enlarged version is displayed at the entrance of the Ukiha City Library.
Local folklore states that numerous earthenware vessels and ironware were once stored in the tomb. It is said that the local people would borrow these objects when needed, but when greedy individuals failed to return them, they disappeared.
Another kofun in Fukuoka Prefecture, the {{ill|Ōzuka Kofun (Katsuragawa, Fukuoka)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōzuka Kofun (Katsuragawa, Fukuoka)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Katsuragawa, Fukuoka|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuragawa, Fukuoka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, features murals similar to those of the Shigesada Kofun, including quivers and concentric circles. Decorated kofun of this type are concentrated in northern Kyushu.

== Shigesada Kofun == [[File:Shigesada Kofun.jpg|thumb|Information board explaining the Shigesada Kofun. The top left shows a quiver (yuki), while the bottom left shows concentric circles.]] {{ill|Shigesada Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigesada Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} located on the left bank of the {{ill|Chikugo River|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is part of the {{ill|Asada Kofun Group|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asada Kofun Group|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and its construction is dated to the latter half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/6th century|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. On March 8, 1922 ({{ill|Taishō era|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 11), it was designated a {{ill|National Historic Site (Japan)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Historic Site (Japan)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} along with the nearby Kusuna Kofun.

The chamber walls are decorated with red and green pigments depicting {{ill|quivers (yuki)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/quivers (yuki)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (containers for carrying arrows), concentric circles, and fern-like motifs. In the rear chamber, quivers and a few concentric circles dominate, while the front chamber primarily features concentric circles, indicating a distinct pattern distribution between the two sections. These murals, composed of quivers and concentric circles, are what were interpreted as "Chikushi Characters."
The inscriptions and murals have been damaged due to various factors. During World War II, the tomb was used as an air raid shelter, and in the postwar period, a surge of interest in archaeology led to increased foot traffic, further eroding the markings. In 1962 (Shōwa 37), Tokyo University of the Arts professor Kusaka Hakkō created a restoration drawing (partially reconstructed). Copies of this restoration are preserved at the Ukiha City Museum of History and Folklore, and an enlarged version is displayed at the entrance of the Ukiha City Library.
Local folklore states that numerous earthenware vessels and ironware were once stored in the tomb. It is said that the local people would borrow these objects when needed, but when greedy individuals failed to return them, they disappeared.
Another kofun in Fukuoka Prefecture, the {{ill|Ōzuka Kofun (Katsuragawa, Fukuoka)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōzuka Kofun (Katsuragawa, Fukuoka)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Katsuragawa, Fukuoka|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuragawa, Fukuoka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, features murals similar to those of the Shigesada Kofun, including quivers and concentric circles. Decorated kofun of this type are concentrated in northern Kyushu.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:筑紫文字]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11602924]] Wikidata name: Tsukushi script [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Chikushi Moji.jpg|thumb|Illustration of the mural in the Shigesada Kofun]] {{ill|Tsukushi script|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukushi script|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (つくしもじ) are a form of [[:en:Jindai moji|Jindai moji]] said to be inscribed on the stone chamber of {{ill|Shigesada Kofun|ja|重定古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigesada Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ukiha, Fukuoka|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukiha, Fukuoka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Japan. They are also referred to as Shigesada Cave Ancient Characters (Shigesada Sekkutsu Koji, '''重定石窟古字''').
== Overview == {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirata Atsutane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} recorded these characters in his 1819 ({{ill|Bunsei|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunsei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 2) book *{{ill|Shinji Hinomibumi-den|ja|神字日文伝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinji Hinomibumi-den|1=Shinji Hinomibumi-den|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589447}}* (神字日文伝), listing them as "Stone Cave Characters of Chikugo Province." According to Hirata, there was a local tradition that referred to them as "characters from the age of the gods." However, he noted, *"It is difficult to ascertain whether they are truly characters."* The drawings of these inscriptions published in his work were originally obtained from a scholar of the {{ill|Kurume Domain|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurume Domain|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, passed on to Hirata through the Kokugaku scholar {{ill|Yashiro Hirokata|ja|屋代弘賢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashiro Hirokata|1=Yashiro Hirokata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6538130}}.
Due to Hirata's influence, interest in the inscriptions grew. When {{ill|Inō Tadataka|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inō Tadataka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} visited Chikugo Province for surveying, he also investigated the Shigesada Kofun, conducting a detailed examination. This research, along with that of the Kurume Domain scholars, remains a valuable resource due to the scarcity of early documentation on decorated kofun.
On April 22, 1825 (Bunsei 8, 3rd month, 5th day), Kurume Domain retainers Murakami Yasutoshi and Hayakawa Issei created a detailed copy of the inscriptions. This illustration was later included in the work *{{ill|Nihon Kodai Moji Kō|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kodai Moji Kō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}* by the Kokugaku scholar {{ill|Ochiai Naozumi|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochiai Naozumi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Ochiai theorized that the script was formed by combining four types of symbols with eleven additional symbols. He also noted that some dot-like marks resembled the diacritic dots used in {{ill|Siddhaṃ script|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Siddhaṃ script|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, used for indicating long vowels. Additionally, historian {{ill|Kamiya Yoshimichi|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiya Yoshimichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} pointed out in his 1886 (Meiji 19) article *Kodaiji Kō* (*古代文字考*), published in the *Journal of the Tokyo Anthropological Society*, that similar inscriptions were found on a stone from the residence of {{ill|Matsuura Takeshirō|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuura Takeshirō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (formerly part of a castle wall), as well as on the {{ill|Temple bell|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple bell|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of {{ill|Takase Shrine|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takase Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Tonami District, Etchū Province (modern-day {{ill|Nanto, Toyama|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto, Toyama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
The *{{ill|Takenouchi Documents|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Documents|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}* mention these inscriptions as "Yasoyo Characters" (ヤソヨ文字). According to *Shindai no Bankokushi* (神代の万国史) by {{ill|Takenouchi Yoshimiya|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Yoshimiya|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, these characters were created during the reign of the 22nd emperor of the Ninth Ancient Dynasty. *Nippon Ji no Hakkutsu* (につぽん字の発掘) by {{ill|Sakai Yoshio|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Yoshio|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} claims they originated in the era of Emperor *Amanohihirumotoashikabikikami*, the first emperor of humanity. (For more information on the emperors mentioned in the Takenouchi Documents, see {{ill|Takenouchi Documents' List of Emperors|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Documents' List of Emperors|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.)
Modern research considers these "Chikushi Characters" to be murals rather than syllabary-based writing, similar to the case of the so-called {{ill|Temiya Cave inscriptions|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temiya Cave inscriptions|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hokkaido. {{ill|Hara Masaru|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Masaru|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in *Illustrated Guide to Shindai Characters* (*図説神代文字入門*), states: *"The attempts of Hirata and Ochiai were, in a way, akin to modern archaeology, as they sought to decipher messages left by ancient people."*
== Shigesada Kofun == [[File:Shigesada Kofun.jpg|thumb|Information board explaining the Shigesada Kofun. The top left shows a quiver (yuki), while the bottom left shows concentric circles.]] {{ill|Shigesada Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigesada Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} located on the left bank of the {{ill|Chikugo River|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is part of the {{ill|Asada Kofun Group|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asada Kofun Group|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and its construction is dated to the latter half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/6th century|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. On March 8, 1922 ({{ill|Taishō era|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 11), it was designated a {{ill|National Historic Site (Japan)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Historic Site (Japan)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} along with the nearby Kusuna Kofun.
The front part of the mound has been lost, so its current length is 51 meters, but it is estimated to have originally been around 80 meters. The rear circular section has a diameter of 44 meters and a height of 8.5 meters. The rear section contains a stone chamber measuring 17 meters in length and 3.8 meters in height, connected to the exterior by a 6-meter-long passage. The chamber is notable for its use of massive andesite stones, with single large slabs forming the back wall, side walls, ceiling, and stone shelves.
The chamber walls are decorated with red and green pigments depicting {{ill|quivers (yuki)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/quivers (yuki)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (containers for carrying arrows), concentric circles, and fern-like motifs. In the rear chamber, quivers and a few concentric circles dominate, while the front chamber primarily features concentric circles, indicating a distinct pattern distribution between the two sections. These murals, composed of quivers and concentric circles, are what were interpreted as "Chikushi Characters."
The inscriptions and murals have been damaged due to various factors. During World War II, the tomb was used as an air raid shelter, and in the postwar period, a surge of interest in archaeology led to increased foot traffic, further eroding the markings. In 1962 (Shōwa 37), Tokyo University of the Arts professor Kusaka Hakkō created a restoration drawing (partially reconstructed). Copies of this restoration are preserved at the Ukiha City Museum of History and Folklore, and an enlarged version is displayed at the entrance of the Ukiha City Library.
Local folklore states that numerous earthenware vessels and ironware were once stored in the tomb. It is said that the local people would borrow these objects when needed, but when greedy individuals failed to return them, they disappeared.
Another kofun in Fukuoka Prefecture, the {{ill|Ōzuka Kofun (Katsuragawa, Fukuoka)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōzuka Kofun (Katsuragawa, Fukuoka)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Katsuragawa, Fukuoka|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuragawa, Fukuoka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, features murals similar to those of the Shigesada Kofun, including quivers and concentric circles. Decorated kofun of this type are concentrated in northern Kyushu.

== Shigesada Kofun == [[File:Shigesada Kofun.jpg|thumb|Information board explaining the Shigesada Kofun. The top left shows a quiver (yuki), while the bottom left shows concentric circles.]] {{ill|Shigesada Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigesada Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} located on the left bank of the {{ill|Chikugo River|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Local folklore states that numerous earthenware vessels and ironware were once stored in the tomb. It is said that the local people would borrow these objects when needed, but when greedy individuals failed to return them, they disappeared.
Another kofun in Fukuoka Prefecture, the {{ill|Ōzuka Kofun (Katsuragawa, Fukuoka)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōzuka Kofun (Katsuragawa, Fukuoka)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Katsuragawa, Fukuoka|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuragawa, Fukuoka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, features murals similar to those of the Shigesada Kofun, including quivers and concentric circles. Decorated kofun of this type are concentrated in northern Kyushu.
== References == * {{ill|Ochiai Naozumi|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochiai Naozumi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, *Nihon Kodai Moji Kō* (日本古代文字考), Yoshikawa Hanshichi, Komakisha, 1888. {{NDL|53013099}} * {{ill|Ago Kiyohiko|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ago Kiyohiko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, *Nihon Shindai Moji: Kodai Waji Sōkan* (日本神代文字-古代和字総観), Tairiku Shobō, 1975. * Ago Kiyohiko, *Nihon Shindai Moji Kenkyū Genten* (日本神代文字研究原典), Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-404-02328-5 * {{ill|Hara Masaru (writer)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Masaru (writer)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, *Illustrated Guide to Shindai Characters: Read, Write, and Use* (図説神代文字入門-読める書ける使える), Being Net Press, 2007. ISBN 978-4-434-10165-6 * {{ill|Ōtsuka Hatsushige|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsuka Hatsushige|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kobayashi Saburō (archaeologist)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Saburō (archaeologist)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Kumano Masaya (eds.), *Nihon Kofun Daijiten* (日本古墳大辞典), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1989. ISBN 978-4-490-10260-4 * {{ill|Shiomi Keiji|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiomi Keiji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, *The Mystery of Decorated Kofun* (装飾古墳のふしぎ), Ashi Shobō.
